prompt: It can reasonably be inferred that the delay between a bat's call and its echo tells the bat about an object's

choices.text: distance
choices.rationale: Correct. The passage says the delay lets the bat judge how far away an object is.

choices.text: color
choices.rationale: Color is never linked to the echoes.

choices.text: weight
choices.rationale: Weight is not part of what the echo reveals.

choices.text: temperature
choices.rationale: Temperature is not mentioned.

explanation: The call-to-echo delay corresponds to distance.

stimulus.title: Seeing With Sound
stimulus.text: A bat hunting in pitch darkness does not bump into things. It calls out, often at pitches too high for humans to hear, and listens for the echoes that bounce back. From the delay between call and echo, the bat judges how far away an object is; from subtle shifts in the returning sound, it can tell whether the object is moving toward it or away. A moth's fluttering wing returns a different echo than a still leaf. So precise is this sense that some bats can snatch an insect from the air in total darkness. The world the bat perceives is built entirely of sound, a picture painted with echoes.

prompt: It can reasonably be inferred that the angle of the waggle run is meaningful because it

choices.text: reveals the particular color of the flowers that were found
choices.rationale: Color is not encoded by the dance.

choices.text: relates the direction of the food to the position of the sun
choices.rationale: Correct. The angle against gravity encodes direction relative to the sun.

choices.text: measures the age of the individual bee that is dancing
choices.rationale: The bee's age is irrelevant to the dance.

choices.text: indicates the current temperature inside the bees' hive
choices.rationale: Temperature is not mentioned.

explanation: The angle's meaning comes from its relation to the sun's direction.

stimulus.title: Dances in the Dark
stimulus.text: A foraging honeybee that finds a rich patch of flowers cannot simply tell the hive where to go. Instead, it dances. On the vertical comb, in darkness, the bee runs a straight line while waggling its body, then loops back to repeat the move. The angle of that straight run, measured against the pull of gravity, encodes the direction of the flowers relative to the sun. The length of the run signals distance. Watching bees crowd around and follow, researchers realized they were seeing a kind of symbolic language, one that passes precise information without sight, sound, or a single spoken word.

prompt: It can reasonably be inferred that fog harvesting is most practical in places that have

choices.text: long, very cold winters lasting much of the year
choices.rationale: Cold winters are not mentioned as a requirement.

choices.text: frequent fog together with steady, reliable winds
choices.rationale: Correct. The passage says it works only where fog is frequent and winds steady.

choices.text: heavy rainfall spread evenly throughout the whole year
choices.rationale: Such places would not need fog harvesting as much.

choices.text: no moving air or wind of any kind at any time
choices.rationale: Steady winds are required, so 'no wind' is wrong.

explanation: Suitability depends on frequent fog and steady wind.

stimulus.title: Catching Water From Air
stimulus.text: In a few dry coastal regions, drinking water can be harvested from fog. Tall mesh nets are strung across hillsides where moist air rolls in from the sea. As the fog drifts through, droplets cling to the mesh, merge, and trickle down into troughs and storage tanks. A single large net can collect many liters on a good day, enough to supplement a village's supply. The method is not a cure-all: it works only where fog is frequent and winds are steady, and the nets need upkeep. Yet in the right place, it turns a passing cloud into a dependable resource, using little more than gravity and patience.

prompt: It can reasonably be inferred that sea otters help kelp forests by

choices.text: slowing the rapid growth of the kelp near the surface
choices.rationale: Otters do not slow kelp growth; their loss harms the kelp.

choices.text: keeping the population of kelp-grazing urchins in check
choices.rationale: Correct. When otters vanish, urchins multiply and graze the kelp down, so otters limit urchins.

choices.text: providing the sunlight that the growing kelp needs
choices.rationale: Sunlight comes from the sun, not from otters.

choices.text: anchoring the giant kelp firmly to the rocky seafloor
choices.rationale: The kelp anchors itself; otters do not anchor it.

explanation: Otters help by controlling the urchins that would otherwise destroy the kelp.

stimulus.title: Forests Beneath the Waves
stimulus.text: Off some cool, rocky coasts grow forests that no hiker will ever walk through. Giant kelp, anchored to the seafloor, can stretch dozens of meters toward the surface, swaying in the current like trees in wind. These kelp forests shelter fish, snails, and otters, much as a woodland shelters birds and deer. The kelp grows astonishingly fast, sometimes half a meter in a single day, drinking sunlight near the surface. But the forest is fragile. Where otters vanish, sea urchins multiply and graze the kelp down to bare rock, leaving a barren the divers call an urchin desert. A single missing animal can unmake a whole forest.

prompt: It can reasonably be inferred that the coral record is valuable mainly because it

choices.text: shows exactly which species of fish lived nearby each year
choices.rationale: Nearby fish species are never mentioned.

choices.text: preserves data from times before people kept measurements
choices.rationale: Correct. The passage stresses conditions recorded 'before anyone thought to measure them.'

choices.text: remains perfectly readable without any special training
choices.rationale: The passage calls reading it a challenge requiring effort.

choices.text: can be created quickly in a laboratory whenever needed
choices.rationale: Coral grows over centuries; it is not made quickly in a lab.

explanation: Its value lies in capturing conditions from before instrument records existed.

stimulus.title: The Coral That Keeps Time
stimulus.text: A coral skeleton grows in fine bands, much like the rings of a tree. In bright, warm months the coral lays down thick layers; in cooler, dimmer months the layers thin. By slicing a coral and counting these bands, researchers can read a record of past seasons stretching back centuries. Even better, the chemistry trapped in each band hints at the water's temperature when it formed. So a single old coral can serve as a kind of diary, written not in words but in stone, recording conditions long before anyone thought to measure them. The challenge is learning to read a language no one designed to be read.

prompt: It can reasonably be inferred that seeds wear hooks in order to

choices.text: protect the seeds from being eaten by any animal
choices.rationale: Hooks help seeds catch a ride, not avoid being eaten.

choices.text: catch onto animals that carry them to new places
choices.rationale: Correct. The passage says hooked seeds snag passing animals and hitch a ride.

choices.text: help the seeds spin like helicopters in the wind
choices.rationale: Spinning like a helicopter describes winged seeds, not hooked ones.

choices.text: make the seeds heavier so they fall straight down
choices.rationale: Hooks aid travel, not added weight to fall straight down.

explanation: Hooks let seeds catch onto animals for transport.

stimulus.title: Travelers Without Legs
stimulus.text: A plant cannot walk, yet its seeds must somehow leave the shadow of the parent and find open ground. Evolution has answered this need with a surprising range of tricks. Some seeds grow papery wings and spin away on the wind like tiny helicopters. Others wear hooks that snag the fur of passing animals, hitching a ride for miles. Still others hide inside sweet fruit, counting on an animal to eat them and carry them off, depositing them far away. Each method is a kind of bargain struck with the world around the plant. The seed that travels farthest is often the one with the best chance to grow where the parent's shade cannot reach.

prompt: It can reasonably be inferred that public voting was a problem chiefly because it

choices.text: prevented neighbors from ever discussing politics
choices.rationale: Public voting did not stop political discussion.

choices.text: required voters to travel long distances to vote
choices.rationale: Travel distance is never mentioned.

choices.text: let powerful people see and punish how others voted
choices.rationale: Correct. The passage says open voting invited pressure and fear of retaliation.

choices.text: made the counting of votes slow and unreliable
choices.rationale: Vote-counting speed is not discussed.

explanation: The danger of public voting was exposure to pressure and punishment.

stimulus.title: The Secret Ballot
stimulus.text: It is easy to assume voting has always been private, but for a long time it was not. In many places people voted aloud or by a show of hands, in full view of employers, landlords, and neighbors. That openness invited pressure: a worker might think twice before voting against a boss who could see the choice. Reformers pushed for a printed ballot, marked alone and dropped in a box, so that no one need fear retaliation for an honest vote. The change seemed minor, a mere matter of paper, yet it shifted real power toward the ordinary voter, who could now decide without an audience.

prompt: It can reasonably be inferred that before locks existed, inland water travel was

choices.text: faster than any later canal-based travel
choices.rationale: No claim is made that earlier travel was faster.

choices.text: carried out mostly by very large ocean ships
choices.rationale: Ocean ships are never mentioned.

choices.text: preferred by traders over every overland route
choices.rationale: Trader preferences for routes are not discussed.

choices.text: limited by differences in a river's elevation
choices.rationale: Correct. The passage says elevation 'blocked boats from traveling far inland.'

explanation: The opening establishes elevation as the obstacle locks later removed.

stimulus.title: Stairs for Boats
stimulus.text: Rivers do not run level, and for centuries that simple fact blocked boats from traveling far inland. The lock changed things. A lock is a chamber with gates at each end; a boat enters, the gates close, and water is let in or drained out until the boat rises or falls to the next stretch of river. String several locks together, and a vessel can climb a hillside the way a person climbs a staircase. The engineering is humble, relying on nothing more exotic than gravity and patience, yet it knit distant towns into trading partners and turned shallow rivers into highways of commerce.
